copy.title: Tigers hammer Rays. White Sox dominate Twins. Knicks host Spurs tomorrow.
copy.keyTakeaway: Tigers and White Sox rolled today while the NBA Finals tip off tomorrow. Tech stocks sold off across the board. God of War lands on PS5 this week.

todaysHits.sports: Tigers dominate Tampa. White Sox shut out Minnesota. Orioles-Red Sox still going.
todaysHits.markets: Tech takes a hit. SPY down 0.7%, NVDA -3.6%.
todaysHits.golf: Brian Campbell leads early at Memorial Tournament.
todaysHits.f1: Monaco Grand Prix roars this weekend.
todaysHits.worldcup: World Cup 2026 kicks off June 11. Nine days away.
todaysHits.culture: God of War Laufey hits PS5. Peabo Bryson, R&B legend, dead at 75.

lead.headline: White Sox shut out Twins again. Minnesota's offense vanished.
lead.whatHappened: Chicago walked into Minnesota and blanked them 8-0 for the second straight dominant performance. The Twins' bats stayed silent while the White Sox pitching staff was nearly untouchable.
lead.whyBullet1: Minnesota's offense has completely disappeared in back-to-back games, which signals something is broken at the plate right now.
lead.whyBullet2: Chicago keeps proving they can travel and execute on the road without letting their opponent scratch even a single run.
lead.whatToSay: The Twins have a serious problem. You don't get shut out twice in a row without something being fundamentally wrong with how you're approaching the at-bat.

sportsOther: Tigers walked into Tampa and dismantled them.
Red Sox buried Baltimore early, never looked back.
White Sox shutout the Twins—complete domination.

markets.mood: Tech took a beating today—NVDA and MSFT both down over 3%—while the 10-year Treasury yield climbed 0.8%, signaling that bond traders are pricing in stickier inflation or higher rates ahead.
markets.whyBullet1: When Treasury yields spike, it makes future corporate earnings worth less in today's dollars. That hits growth stocks hardest, which is why the Nasdaq barely moved while mega-cap tech got hammered.
markets.whyBullet2: The market's watching inflation data and Fed commentary through the rest of the week. Any signal that the Fed's done raising rates could reverse this sell-off fast. Thursday's jobs report will matter too.
markets.bringUp: Bitcoin dropped 5.3% today, the biggest mover on the board. It's usually the first thing to sell when traders get nervous about the macro picture.

golf.headline: Campbell, Coody, Fox tied at Muirfield Village to start Memorial
golf.whyCare1: Muirfield is Jack Nicklaus's course and one of the toughest tests on tour — winning here means something different than winning elsewhere.
golf.whyCare2: This is a FedEx Cup event, so points matter now; the field knows every stroke counts toward the playoffs.
golf.watchFor: Whether Campbell can hold the lead through 72 holes — he's a solid player but Muirfield tends to separate pretenders from contenders down the stretch.
golf.whatToSay: Campbell's got the lead at Muirfield, but that course eats guys alive. Check back Sunday to see if he can actually hold it.

culture.topic: NBC Cancels The Hunting Party After Two Seasons
culture.whatHappened: NBC pulled the plug on The Hunting Party, ending the show after its second season.
culture.whyItMatters: Another mid-tier drama didn't stick around long enough to build a real audience, which means networks are getting more trigger-happy with cancellations.
culture.whatToSay: Yeah, I watched like two episodes. Not shocked it didn't make it.

culture.topic: Peabo Bryson, R&B Legend, Dies at 75
culture.whatHappened: Peabo Bryson, the R&B singer behind the Beauty and the Beast and A Whole New World duets, passed away at 75.
culture.whyItMatters: That guy soundtracked your entire childhood if you grew up in the 90s, and his voice defined what Disney songs could actually be.
culture.whatToSay: Peabo Bryson just died. That guy's voice was everywhere when we were kids.

culture.whatHappened: Four episodes, each shot in a single continuous take — no cuts, no edits, no hiding.

copy.finalSharpTake: Tech got smacked harder than stocks today—NVDA and MSFT both down over three percent while SPY barely budged—which means the rotation out of mega-cap AI plays is real. Bitcoin's already down five percent, which tells you institutional money isn't just trimming positions, it's walking away from risk entirely. The World Cup opens in eight days and nobody's thinking about summer portfolios yet. This is a get-defensive week, not a hunt-for-bargains week.
